segments.s316-g41: This is a relative/subordinate clause introduced by که (’that/who’) referring back to a previous noun; اکنون is an adverb 'now'. بدین is the fused preposition + demonstrative (ب = به 'into/to' + دین = این 'this' in the given tokenization) meaning 'into this'. تنگ غار is a noun phrase 'narrow cave' (adjective before noun). اندری is an archaic/poetic locative (اندر + ی) meaning 'within/inside', reinforcing location.
segments.s316-g42: گریزان is an adjectival/participial form 'fleeing' or 'having fled' describing the subject; به here is the directional preposition 'into/towards'. سنگین حصار is a compound noun 'heavy/strong enclosure or fortress'. The final اندری again marks interior location ('inside'). The sequence places the participle before the direction and goal, a typical poetic word order.
segments.s316-g43: به ترکی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in Turkish' (here 'in the Turkish language'). چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; این ناله is the direct object 'this cry/lament'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. هوم is a proper name (here the person who hears).
segments.s316-g44: پرستش is the noun object 'worship' (what is abandoned). رها کرد is a compound verb meaning 'let go/abandoned' (رها + کرد, past tense). و is the coordinating 'and'. بگذاشت بوم is a verbal phrase where بگذاشت (past) means 'left/abandoned' in this context and بوم is 'homeland/abode' — together 'he left his homeland.' Note classical usage allows repeated verbs meaning 'abandoned X and left Y.'
segments.s316-g45: چنین گفت is 'he said thus' (چنین = thus/so; گفت = said — past). کاین is a fused relative/complementizer (که + این) introducing reported content 'that this...'. ناله هنگام خواب is a noun phrase 'the cry at the time of sleep' (هنگام = 'at the time of', خواب = 'sleep').
segments.s316-g46: نباشد here is a negative present/subjunctive-like form meaning 'is not / could not be' (classical Persian uses نباشد in formulas like 'نباشد مگر...' to mean 'there is none except...'). مگر means 'except/unless' and آن افراسیاب names the only possible source: 'except that Afrasiab.'
segments.s316-g47: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. اندیشه شد is a common classical idiom 'a thought arose / it occurred' (اندیشه + شد, past). بر دلش is the prepositional phrase 'upon his heart' (بر = on/upon, دلش = his heart). بر درست here is an adverbial/directional phrase; درست is used in the sense 'right/proper' and with بر gives 'toward/rightly' (poetic phrasing meaning he resolved/fixed upon action).
segments.s316-g48: در غار تاریک is a locative phrase 'in the dark cave' (در = in, غار = cave, تاریک = dark). چندی is an adverb 'for a while'. بجست is a past verb here meaning 'he searched/searched about' (classical past form; it can also mean 'leapt' in other contexts, but here it fits 'sought/groped around').
segments.s316-g97: بدانست is past simple, 3rd-person singular (he knew/realized). کان here is a classical/poetic clause-marker introducing a reported content ('that'); مرد پرهیزگار is a noun (مرد) followed by its adjective (پرهیزگار) — the adjective comes after the noun, as is normal in Persian poetry. The whole segment functions as the subject/content of 'he realized'.
segments.s316-g98: ببخشود is past 3rd-person singular; in context it means 'granted' or 'gave in response' rather than simply 'forgave'. بر + noun expresses the ground or occasion (here 'on account of' / 'because of' the lament). ناله شهریار = 'the king's lament' (possessor follows the possessed in this poetic phrasing; the ezafe is elided). Together the clause says that the pious man granted (the request) because of the king's lament.
segments.s316-g99: بپیچید is past 3rd-person (it/he/she) 'turned' or 'twisted'. و is the simple coordinating 'and'. ز او = 'from him/it' (ز is the poetic form of 'from'), خویشتن is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun 'itself/himself', and درکشید (here) means 'drew in' or 'withdrew' (literally 'pulled in'). The word order places the source (ز او) before the reflexive object and the verb, typical of Persian clause ordering.
segments.s316-g100: به دریا = 'into/toward the sea' (به + noun marking direction). درون جست is a compound: درون 'within' + جست (past of 'to spring/jump') = 'sprang inward' / 'leapt into the depths'. و شد ناپدید uses شد ('became') plus the adjective ناپدید 'vanished' to mean 'and disappeared' (became invisible/vanished). The sequence is narrative past: action, then result (disappearance).
segments.s316-g9: ندید is the past negative 'did not see' (simple past). از + برش functions as a deictic phrase 'from it / of its surface' (برش here = بر + ش, 'on/over it'); جای پرواز is a noun phrase 'a place/spot for flying/escape' with پرواز as a verbal-noun 'flight'; باز modifies جای (poetic 'open/clear'), so the whole segment means 'he saw no open place from it for flight.'
segments.s316-g10: نه is the coordinating negative 'nor'. زیرش = زیر + ـش 'beneath it'; پی here means 'track/path' or 'way' (i.e. a route beneath it). شیر and گراز are nouns 'lion' and 'wild boar'; آن before گراز is the demonstrative 'that'. The segment reads 'nor beneath it a track for the lion and that boar.'
segments.s316-g11: خورش برد is a verbal sequence 'he took (خورش = food/dish; برد here functions as 'took' though the provided noun senses do not list the verbal use).' وز is an archaic/poetic connective here meaning 'from/owing to' (introducing cause), followed by بیم جان 'fear for (his) life' (بیم = fear, جان = life). جای ساخت is 'he made/created a place' with ساخت as past 'built/made.'
segments.s316-g12: به غار اندرون is a locative phrase 'into/within the cave' (به + غار + اندرون). جای بالای ساخت is 'he built a raised/upper place' (بالای = 'above/upper', جای = 'place', ساخت = past 'made/built'). Word order is typical: locative first, then the clause describing what he built.
segments.s316-g13: ز هر شهر دور = a poetic locative 'far from every city' (ز = 'from', هر = 'every', شهر = 'city', دور = 'far'). و به نزدیک آب continues the locative 'and near the water' (به + نزدیک = 'toward/near', آب = 'water'). The coordinate structure contrasts distance from settlements with proximity to water.
segments.s316-g14: که introduces a relative clause 'where/which'. خوانی is used in the sense 'to call/summon' (perfect/past narrative function here). ورا = 'to him' (accusative/dative clitic). هنگ can mean 'time/occasion' and here signals 'at that time/occasion'; افراسیاب is the agent (Afrasiab). The clause thus says 'which Afrasiab at that time summoned him.'
segments.s316-g15: همی بود is an imperfective past habitative 'he was / he remained' (همی + بود gives ongoing or continued past). چندی = 'for a while'; به هنگ اندرون = 'in that time/at that moment, inside' (به + هنگ + اندرون gives a temporal-locative phrase). The segment describes duration and location of his remaining.
segments.s316-g16: ز کرده پشیمان = 'regretful of the deed(s) done' (ز = 'from/on account of', کرده = 'what has been done/deed', پشیمان = 'regretful'). و links the next phrase: دل پر ز خون = 'a heart full of blood' (دل = heart, پر = 'full (of)', ز خون = 'of blood' — a poetic way to say his heart was filled with blood/bloodshed).
segments.s316-g1: از آن پس is a temporal phrase: از 'from' + آن 'that' + پس 'after/then' = 'from that time on'. چنان بد که introduces a result clause ('so/so bad that'), with که marking the following clause. افراسیاب is the subject placed after the subordinate marker (poetic word order).
segments.s316-g2: همی بود is a past narrative pattern: همی (iterative/continuous adverb) + بود (past of 'to be') = 'he was/kept being'. هر جای = 'in every place/wherever'. بی‌خورد و خواب is a compound nominal phrase 'without eating and without sleep' describing his deprived condition; the copula has already occurred (بود).
segments.s316-g3: نه ... و نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor'. ایمن به جان literally 'safe with respect to the life/soul' (prepositional phrase: ایمن + به + جان). نه تن سودمند negates the second coordinated phrase: تن 'body' modified by سودمند 'useful/beneficial'—here functioning to mean 'sound/healthy' (poetic usage).
segments.s316-g4: This is a verbless descriptive clause: هراسان همیشه 'always terrified' (هراسان as predicate adjective, همیشه adverb). ز بیم گزند = 'from fear of harm' with ز (poetic form of از) marking cause and گزند 'harm' the object of بیم 'fear' (fear of harm).
segments.s316-g5: همی ... بجست is an iterative past construction: همی (continuous/ongoing) + بجست (past of بجستن 'to seek') = 'he kept seeking'. از جهان جایگاهی = 'a place apart from the world' with از marking separation and جایگاهی (indefinite place) the direct object.
segments.s316-g6: که introduces a relative/purpose clause 'that/so that'. باشد here is the optative/subjunctive sense 'would be' or 'might be' (classic Persian uses باشد after که to express wished-for or possible state). به جان ایمن و تن‌درست = 'safe in soul and sound in body' with به marking the domain (with respect to the soul) and the adjective تن‌درست 'physically sound'.
segments.s316-g7: به نزدیک is an adverbial phrase 'nearby'. بردع is a noun in an ablative/instrumental sense 'with/under a cloak' (poetic placement), followed by یکی غار 'a certain cave' (یکی before the noun = 'a certain'). بود is the past copula placed at the end as usual.
segments.s316-g8: This phrase identifies the cave's position and condition: سر کوه غار = 'the cave at the mountain's summit' (سر as 'top/summit' governing کوه). از جهان نابسود = 'not benefiting from the world' or 'untouched/unprofitable with regard to the world', where از marks relation/cause and نابسود (negated 'پ سود') means 'fruitless / not benefiting'.
segments.s316-g33: همه (quantifier) precedes the coordinated nouns ترک و چین to mean "all the Turks and the Chinese." زیر فرمان تو is a prepositional phrase meaning "under your command/control," with زیر + noun + possessive pronoun تو (placed after the noun in poetic word order). There is no copular verb (هستند) — the predicate is implied, a common poetic omission.
segments.s316-g34: رسیده is a perfect/participial past form used predicatively: "have reached/arrived." It takes the preceding group (همه ترک و چین) as its subject. به هر جای is a prepositional phrase "to every place," and پیمان تو is a genitive construction where the demonstrative/possessive تو follows پیمان in the compressed, poetic order: "the places of your covenant/pact."
segments.s316-g35: یکی is an indefinite "a certain / one" modifying غار, so یکی غار داری = "you have a certain/a single cave." داری is present-second-person "you have." به بهره is an adverbial/prepositional phrase "for use/for pleasure/for a share," and به چنگ here means "with a (musical) harp" (چنگ = harp) — the second به marks accompaniment or instrument.
segments.s316-g36: کجات is an interrogative adverb (poetic) meaning "from where / whence" and introduces a rhetorical question about whereabouts. آن is the demonstrative "that," and the following coordination سر تاج و مردان جنگ lists items whose origin/whereabouts are questioned. سر here has the sense of "chief/head/leading part," and مردان جنگ means "warriors/men of war." The copula or verb is omitted for rhetorical force.
segments.s316-g37: کجات (whence) again launches a rhetorical question; آن همه (lit. "that much/all that") intensifies the following nouns. زور و مردانگی are coordinated nouns meaning "strength and manliness/bravery," standing as the things whose origin is being asked. As before, the verb is omitted (implied).
segments.s316-g38: This segment continues the list of qualities with asyndeton: دلیری (bravery), نیروی (strength/force) and فرزانگی (wisdom). Each noun is in the nominative/absolute form and linked by و; they function as parallel items governed by the interrogative کجات from the previous clause.
segments.s316-g39: کجات آن introduces another rhetorical question. بزرگی (greatness), تخت (throne) and کلاه (hat/headgear) are listed as the regal attributes being queried. The demonstrative آن precedes the whole list; the copula is again omitted for poetic effect.
segments.s316-g40: A parallel interrogative list: کجات آن (whence that) modifies بر و بوم (an idiomatic pair meaning realm/land/homeland — بوم is the core "homeland"), and چندان سپاه means "so many / that many armies/troops," with چندان as an emphatic quantifier preceding سپاه. Again no verb is stated; the speaker rhetorically asks where these things have gone or come from.
segments.s316-g17: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خونریز (an adjective) and گردد (the verb 'become') form the predicate 'becomes bloodstained'; classical Persian often places the adjective/attribute before the verb. سر سرفراز is the noun phrase 'the proud/exalted head' (سر = head, سرفراز = proud/victorious) with the epithet after the noun.
segments.s316-g18: به تخت کیان is a prepositional phrase 'on/onto the throne of the Kians' (به here marking the locus). برنماند is the past participial/resultant form 'remained' (used here in an existential/result sense), and دراز ('long') qualifies the duration; the clause says a long remaining on the Kayan throne will not endure (classical compression implies lack of long rule after the head is bloodied).
segments.s316-g19: یکی introduces existence here as 'a certain' / 'one' and heads the noun phrase یکی مرد نیک ('a good man'); اندر آن روزگار is the locative 'in that time/age' (اندر = in, آن روزگار = that time). The clause is an existential statement without an explicit copula.
segments.s316-g20: ز تخم فریدون is a lineage phrase 'from the seed/stock of Fereydun' (ز = from, تخم = seed/lineage, فریدون = the mythic progenitor). آموزگار is an appositive 'teacher' or 'moral instructor' describing that man; classical Persian commonly strings such appositives without a linking verb.
segments.s316-g21: پرستار is an agent noun here meaning 'guardian/caretaker' (one who guards or tends). با introduces accompaniment or possession 'with' and links فر (the sacred royal radiance/farr) and برز (exalted status/high rank) to کیان (the Kayan dynasty): 'guardian with the farr and lofty rank of the Kians.'
segments.s316-g22: به هر کار is 'in every matter' (به marking domain, هر کار = every affair). با شاه بسته میان is a compressed predicative phrase: با شاه 'toward/with the king', بسته (past-participle/adjective) 'bound/tied', and میان here gives the relational sense ('to' / 'with' — idiomatically 'firmly attached to the king'); together: 'in every affair he was closely bound to the king.'
segments.s316-g23: پرستشگهش ('his shrine' — پرستشگه + possessive suffix) is the subject; کوه بودی همه is 'was a mountain entirely' with بودی the past copula 'was' and همه stressing 'entirely/wholly'. Classical word order often places the predicate after the subject like this.
segments.s316-g24: ز شادی شده literally 'having been of/with joy' i.e. 'given over to rejoicing' (ز = from/because/of; شادی = joy; شده = become/been made). دور و دور is a reduplicated adverb 'far and far' for emphasis, and از رمه 'from the flock' means 'away from the herds' — together: 'filled with rejoicing, far from the flocks.'
segments.s316-g81: چنین is an adverb placed before the verb meaning “thus/so.” راند is the classical third-person past-form of راندن (“to drive”), so here “he/it drove.” بر سر = “upon the head/top of” (بر + سر) and سپهر بلند is a noun + postpositive adjective (“the high vault/heavens”); the whole prepositional phrase follows the verb to mark location.
segments.s316-g82: که introduces a result/subordinate clause (“so that / such that”). آید is the third-person present (or optative/resultive) of آمدن here rendered as “may/does come.” ز is the poetic preposition “from” (here functioning to mark source/affecting relation, understood idiomatically as “upon me” in context). The coordinated nouns درد و رنج و گزند list the kinds of suffering that will “come.”
segments.s316-g83: ز فرمان یزدان literally “from the command of God” (ز + noun + possessor) marks the source/authority. کسی is an indefinite pronoun; with the negative verb نگذرد it yields the universal negative sense “no one.” نگذرد = ن- + گذرد (third-person present) here means “does not pass/escape.”
segments.s316-g84: وگر is a concessive conditional “and if / even if.” دیده is the noun “eye” (not the participle here). اژدها is “dragon/monster.” بسپرد is the classical form of سپردن (to entrust/hand over); in this conditional it has the sense “were to entrust / should hand over.” The clause expresses a hypothetical concession to strengthen the previous assertion.
segments.s316-g85: ببخشای is an imperative/subjunctive plea meaning “forgive / grant mercy.” بر من is a prepositional phrase = “upon me / to me” (classical usage for addressing mercy toward the speaker). که here introduces the reason/explanation (“for / because”) and بیچاره‌ام is an adjective plus the 1st-person suffix “-am,” “I am wretched / helpless.”
segments.s316-g86: وگر is concessive “and if / even if.” چند in this classical/poetic position works as a softening/adverbial “somewhat / at times / even if” (concessive flavor). بر خود = “against/toward myself.” ستمکاره‌ام is the noun ستمکار (“oppressor / wrong-doer”) with 1st-person suffix –am: “I have been an oppressor (toward) myself.” The clause concedes a possible fault while pleading for pardon.
segments.s316-g87: نبیره is a noun “scion/descendant.” It is followed by the proper name apposition فریدون فرخ (“Fereydun, the Fortunate”) specifying the ancestor. منم is the emphatic copula “I am” (من + enclitic -م). The whole phrase asserts lineage and identity.
segments.s316-g88: ز is the preposition “from.” بند here means “fetter/bond,” and کمندت is کمند (“lasso, snare”) with the 2nd-person possessive suffix -ت (“your snare”). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (classical) marking certainty/continuity (“indeed/ surely / still”) and بگسلم is the first-person singular present/near-future of گسستن (“I will break / I break away”). Together: “I will surely break away from the bonds of your snare.”
segments.s316-g73: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/among' introducing a partitive: 'among the kings of the world'. The following nouns شاهان گیتی form the noun phrase 'kings of the world' and برادر is the head noun ('a brother') modified by the relative clause introduced by که. The clause که کشت uses که as a relative 'who/that' and کشت is a past action 'killed' (elliptical active past relating to that brother).
segments.s316-g74: The clause begins with که linking back to the preceding relative phrase; شد is the past of شدن 'became/was brought to' (here indicating what happened to that person). نیز is an adverb 'also/too'. با + noun is a prepositional phrase 'with/by'; پاک یزدان is a devotional phrase 'the Pure God', and درشت is an adjective meaning 'harsh/violent' describing the manner of the divine action or the character of the divine judgment.
segments.s316-g75: چو is the comparative/simile 'like/as' introducing an example. The proper names اغریرث and نوذر are coordinated by و. نامدار is a postpositive adjective meaning 'renowned/famed' and modifies the coordinated names.
segments.s316-g76: سیاوش is the proper name (subject). The particle که introduces a relative or descriptive clause 'who/that'; بد here means 'ill/unfortunate' and qualifies the following nominal phrase. در جهان is the locative 'in the world', and یادگار is the noun 'memorial/remembrance' — together describing Siawush as an unfortunate remembrance in the world.
segments.s316-g77: تو is the direct addressee 'you' (poetic/thou). خون سر is a noun phrase 'the blood (life) of the head' — here idiomatically 'the blood of (the) innocent' — with سر specifying the kind/importance of the blood. مریز is the negative imperative 'do not pour/ spill' (م- prefix + ریز).
segments.s316-g78: نه begins the second prohibitive 'nor' / 'do not'. اندر is the preposition 'in/within'. بن غار is a compound-like sequence meaning 'the base/root of a cave' (بن = root/foundation, غار = cave). بی‌بن is an adjective 'rootless' or 'of no lineage', and گریز is the noun/verb 'flight/flight-seeking' used here as 'do not flee / do not seek refuge'. The whole unit issues a prohibition against taking refuge in a rootless cave.
segments.s316-g79: بدو is the prepositional/indirect object 'to him' (he said to him). گفت is the verb 'said' (past) though the provided sense list gives noun senses for گفت; here it functions as the past tense verb. کاندر is a fused locative 'that/in which' used as 'in this/that world' and جهان specifies the domain 'the world'; بیگناه is the adjective/noun 'innocent', completing the interrogative framework 'who is innocent in the world?'.
segments.s316-g80: که را forms the interrogative object 'whom' (که = who/which; را = object/definite marker emphasizing the person). دانی is 2nd-person singular present 'do you know'. ای مرد با دستگاه is a vocative address 'O man of apparatus/establishment' — i.e. 'O man of rank or court' — where با دستگاه characterizes the man's station.
segments.s316-g25: "کجا" is the interrogative adverb 'where' placed at the start; the phrase "نام این نامور هوم" is a noun phrase 'the name of this famed Hom' ("نامور" qualifies "هوم"). The past copula "بود" follows the noun phrase, yielding 'where was the name of this famed Hom?'.
segments.s316-g26: "پرستنده" is the subject 'a worshipper/devotee'; the locative phrase "دور از بر و بوم" means 'far from hearth and homeland' with the preposition "از" marking source and "دور" as adjective 'distant'. The clause ends with the past copula "بود" ('was/ lived'), so the whole clause states that the worshipper was far from home.
segments.s316-g27: "یکی" here means 'a certain' (introducing a single item) and combines with "کاخ" 'palace'; "بود" is the past copula 'stood/was'. The locative phrase "اندر آن برز کوه" ('upon that lofty mountain') follows to specify where the palace was, with "برز" modifying "کوه" ('high/lofty mountain').
segments.s316-g28: "بدو" is the pronominal adverb 'to it/to him' referring back to the place; "سخت" is an adverbial intensifier 'very/firmly' modifying "نزدیک" ('near'). The conjunction "و" links the contrasting phrase "دور از گروه" ('far from the company'), where "دور از" expresses separation from the group.
segments.s316-g29: This is a noun phrase formed with a perfect participle followed by the agent: پرستشگهی کرده (having built/made a place of worship) qualifies the following noun پشمینه‌پوش (the wool‑clad one). کرده is the adjectival/perfect participle ‘having done/made’, and پشمینه‌پوش is a compound descriptive noun meaning ‘one wearing wool/pelt’. Word order (participle + noun) is common in poetic Persian to form an attributive phrase.
segments.s316-g30: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'. کافش = کاف + ـش, where ـش is the enclitic possessive 'his', so 'from his palm'. یکی modifies ناله as an indefinite/quantifying 'one/a single' (here 'a single moan'). آمد is simple past 'came' (i.e. 'was heard'), and به گوش is the idiom 'to the ear' meaning 'reached/heard by the ear'. The whole clause means 'from his palm a single moan came to the ear'.
segments.s316-g31: که at the start functions as a relative/explanatory linker 'that' (introducing a clause) and is followed immediately by the vocative شاها ('O king') addressing the addressee. سرا is the noun 'house/dwelling' and is modified postpositively by نامور ('renowned') and the name/epithet مهترا (Mahtara). In classical/poetic Persian adjectives and names commonly follow the head noun.
segments.s316-g32: بزرگان و = 'the great ones and ...' coordinates the following phrase. بر here means 'over/above' and combines with داوران ('judges') while داورا is a nominative/title 'judge' used appositionally — together conveying 'Daura, a judge over judges' or 'the judge above judges'. The phrase as a whole lists who esteems/relates to the dwelling: 'the great and Daura, judge over judges.'
segments.s316-g49: ز + noun puts a source/starting-point before the verb (poetic order): ز کوه = 'from the mountain'. اندر آمد is the verb phrase 'came/entered' (آمد past), here combined with ز to indicate motion out of the mountain; به هنگام خواب is a temporal adjunct 'at the time of sleep' (به + هنگام + noun). Word order is somewhat inverted in classical/Persian-poetic style.
segments.s316-g50: بدید is simple past 'he saw'. آن در = 'that gate/door' (آن determiner before noun). هنگ افراسیاب is a locative/genitive-type phrase (هنگ here = the court/retinue/time associated with Afrasiab) that locates the gate at Afrasiab's place; in classical Persian such modifiers often follow the noun.
segments.s316-g51: بیامد is a past form 'he came' (prefix bi- here is a narrative/poetic variant of آمد). به کردار = 'in the manner of / like' (به + کردار), so به کردار شیر ژیان = 'in the manner of a lion (شیر) — ژیان intensifies or qualifies 'lion' (an archaic epithet). The phrase is a straightforward simile.
segments.s316-g52: ز پشمینه = 'from/out of (his) woolen cloak' (ز as source + پشمینه 'woolen cloak'). بگشاد is a past form of گشودن 'opened' (ب- prefix + گشاد), here transitive 'he opened/let open'. گردی میان in classical diction denotes a rounded opening/space in the middle; this is an older/poetic use of گردی ('a round/ округ/ aperture') and میان = 'middle'.
segments.s316-g53: کمندی (dim. of کمند) = 'a noose/lasso' is the head noun. که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. بر جای زنار داشت = 'he had (it) in the place of a girdle' (بر جای = 'in place of'; زنار = 'girdle'; داشت past 'he had/was wearing'). Classical order places the modifying PP before the verb.
segments.s316-g54: کجا is an interrogative/adversative 'where' (often rhetorical in poetry). در پناه جهاندار داشت = 'did he have (any) refuge in the protection of the sovereign' (در پناه = 'in the shelter/protection of'; جهاندار = 'sovereign, lord of the world'); داشت is past 'had'. The clause questions the possibility of refuge under the ruler.
segments.s316-g55: به هنگ = 'at the moment/at the time' (temporal prepositional phrase). اندرون شد uses شد as an auxiliary/verb 'he went/entered inside' (اندرون = 'inside/within'). گرفت آن به دست is the following main action 'he seized that with his hand' (گرفت past 'took/seized'; آن = 'that' object; به دست instrument 'with the hand'). Classical Persian permits this compact sequencing of verbs.
segments.s316-g56: چو نزدیک شد = 'when he came near' (چو = 'when'; نزدیک شد past 'became/was near'). بازوی او ببست = 'he bound/tied his arm' (بازوی 'arm' + enclitic pronoun او 'his' following the noun; ببست past of بستن used here as 'bound/tied'). Note the object phrase precedes the verb, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s316-g57: همی رفت is a continuous/habitual past: همی + past verb indicates he kept on going. و links the next clause. او را is the object with the definite object marker را (him); پس اندر کشان is an adverbial participial phrase: کشان is a present-participial/agentive form from کشاندن ('dragging'), and پس اندر ('after/inside') shows direction — 'dragging him after/into (it)'.
segments.s316-g58: همی تاخت repeats the continuous adverb + past verb pattern to mean 'he rushed on/kept charging.' با رنج is an instrumental phrase 'with hardship/effort.' چون introduces a simile ('as/like'), and بیهشان (an archaic compound) qualifies the simile (here functioning roughly 'as if bereft/without [something]').
segments.s316-g59: شگفت is an exclamatory remark ('strange'/'wonderful'). ار بمانی is a conditional ('if you remain') with ار = 'if' and بمانی as present/subjunctive form of ماندن. بدین در is a locative phrase 'in/at this place' (بدین = 'with/at this'), and رواست is the copular predicate 'it is fitting/permissible' (روا + است contracted).
segments.s316-g60: هر آن کس که ... introduces a generic relative clause 'anyone who...'. او is the pronoun 'he/one', بر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'over the world', and پادشاست is an older compound form meaning 'is king/ruler' (پادشاه + است fused). The whole relative clause describes the person for whom the preceding statement is fitting.
segments.s316-g61: جز از نیک‌نامی is a prepositional exception phrase 'except from good repute/renown.' نباید is an impersonal modal 'one should not / it must not'; here it governs گزید (the past-stem used as an infinitival/actional form) meaning 'choose', so the clause says essentially 'one must choose nothing except good repute.'
segments.s316-g62: بباید is a modal 'one ought/must' (impersonal). The verbs چمید and چرید follow it as action verbs in past-stem form used to express obligation ('one ought to چمید and one ought to چرید'). The exact meaning of چمید is archaic/uncertain here; چرید is the frequentative/past-stem of چریدن ('to graze/browse/roam').
segments.s316-g63: ز گیتی = 'from the world' (ز is poetic 'from'). یک غار بگزید uses بگزید (past-stem of گزیدن) as an exhortation/imperative-like form 'choose one cave'; راست here is an adverb 'rightly/truly' qualifying the verb. The clause is a general instruction.
segments.s316-g64: چه دانست is a rhetorical question 'who/what does he know' (literally 'what did he know' used as 'who can know'). کان غار = 'that cave' (کان used demonstratively/introducing 'that'). هنگ بلاست: هنگ = 'time/moment' and بلاست is a predicate meaning 'is calamity/affliction' (بلا + است); the whole asks whether that cave might be a time/place of disaster. Several of these words are somewhat archaic/poetic usages.
segments.s316-g65: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. آن شاه را is the object marked by را (definite object marker); هوم is the subject (a personal name) that follows the object here for poetic word order; بازو is the direct object 'arm' that belongs to شاه; ببست is the simple past 3rd-person singular verb 'bound/tied' (here transitive) agreeing with هوم. The clause literally: 'When Haoma bound that king's arm...'.
segments.s316-g66: همی is an adverbial particle giving a progressive or continued meaning ('kept/was'), placed before the verb. بردش is the verb برد with the enclitic object pronoun -ش 'him' (here meaning 'took/carried him'); the exact sense 'to carry/take' is the intended meaning though the provided sense list doesn't show it. از جایگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the place/seat' and نشست is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'sat' (intransitive); syntactically the clause says Haoma took him from his seat/place and then sat (down).
segments.s316-g67: بدو ('to him') is a dative clitic-like adverb meaning 'to him/unto him' and precedes گفت, which here functions as the past tense verb 'said' (the provided senses list treats گفت as 'conversation' but context gives the verbal 'he said'). کای is a vocative particle 'O'; مرد is the noun 'man'; باهوش and باک are adjectives following the noun and joined by و 'and' ('intelligent and fearful/cautious'). The phrase is a vocative address: 'O intelligent and cautious man.'
segments.s316-g68: پرستار here is an epithet meaning 'worshipper/adorer' (not merely 'nurse'); دارنده is a participial noun 'holder, possessor' that governs the following noun یزدان in a genitive-like relation (classical Persian often leaves the ezāfe implicit in poetry); یزدان پاک = 'the pure Yazdan (God)' where پاک is a postpositive adjective modifying یزدان. Together the phrase apposes/praises the addressee: 'worshipper, possessor of the pure Yazdan.'
segments.s316-g69: چه خواهی is a direct interrogative 'what do you want' with خواهی as 2nd-person (present/imperative-bordering) 'you want'. ز introduces the source 'from'; من‌من is a substantive meaning 'muttering, murmuring'; کییم is a first‑person‑plural existential/question 'who are we' (ک + ـیم). در جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world'. Word order is somewhat compressed for poetry: 'What do you want from my muttering? Who are we in the world...'.
segments.s316-g70: نشسته is a participial/past adjective 'sitting' (describing the subjects just mentioned). بدین غار is a prepositional phrase 'in this cave' (بدین = ب + این = 'in/at this' in poetic contraction). با اندهان = 'with (our) thoughts/minds' (با + noun). The whole segment modifies 'we' (کییم): 'we, sitting in this cave with our thoughts.' Note that some token glosses provided do not show the exact poetic forms; syntactically this is a participial location phrase attached to the preceding question.
segments.s316-g71: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said' — the provided senses list treats گفت as 'conversation' but here it is the verb). هوم is the speaker 'Haoma' (personal name). The reported sentence begins: این نه آرام تست = 'this is not calm/peace for you' where نه negates آرام. تست is an archaic poetic/copulative form (contraction of ت + است) functioning as 'is' (the provided senses 'test'/'to test' are not the intended meaning here).
segments.s316-g72: جهانی is the subject 'the world'; سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout/entire'; پر (followed by از) means 'full (of)'; از نام is the prepositional construction 'of names' where نام here carries the sense 'name' → 'fame/reputation' in this context; the final تست again is the archaic copula 'is'. The line states as a predicate: 'The world is entirely full of (fame/names).' Word order places the predicate elements after the subject in the usual Persian manner.
segments.s316-g89: This is an interrogative fronting: کجا ('where') begins the clause. خواهی is the 2nd-person present/future auxiliary ('you will/would') that combines with the main verb برد (here meaning 'take/lead away' in the poetic context) which is placed unusually early; م + را together mark the object 'me' (م = clitic for 'me', را = definite/object marker). بسته خوار is a postnominal adjectival phrase 'bound, despised' describing the speaker; note the poetic split of the object into clitic + را.
segments.s316-g90: نترسی is a negative present/imperative form addressing 'you' ('do not fear' / 'you do not fear'); ز is the ablative/agentive preposition 'from' (poetic z-), so ز یزدان = 'from God' / 'of God'. به‌روز شمار forms a bound phrase meaning 'the day of reckoning' or 'the day of account' (به‌روز = 'of the day', شمار = 'count/reckoning').
segments.s316-g91: بدو گفت is an old/poetic order where بدو ('to him') precedes the verb گفت ('said'), so it means 'he said to him'. هوم‌ای is a vocative ('Haoma, O ...') with the vocative -ای. بد بدگمان are epithets applied to the addressee: بد 'bad/ill' and بدگمان 'mistrustful/suspicious'.
segments.s316-g92: همانا is an emphatic adverb ('truly/indeed'). فراوان here intensifies quantity ('much/for long'). نماندت is a past negative 2nd-person form ('you did not remain' / 'you have little time left' in context), and زمان ('time') completes the idea: literally 'truly, much time did not remain (for you)'.
segments.s316-g93: سخنهات is the plural/possessive form 'your lines/utterances' (here best read as poetic lines/verses). چون introduces a simile 'like/as'. گلستان نوست is a predicate 'is a new (fresh) rose-garden' — نوست is the poetic contraction of 'نو است' ('is new').
segments.s316-g94: ترا here is split orthographically into ت + را: ت is the 2nd-person enclitic (your/thee) and را the object/topic marker; together they mark the person related to هوش ('wit/intelligence'). The copular/possessive clause 'هوش … کیخسروست' means 'your wit is at/in the hand (power) of Kay Khosrow' — بر دست ('in the hand/power of') indicates who holds that wit. کیخسروست is a compound predicate meaning 'belongs to Kay Khosrow / is Kay Khosrow's'.
segments.s316-g95: بپیچید is a past intransitive/preterite form of پیچید ('turned/twisted') with the prefix ب- often found in epic/poetic narrative. دل هوم را is the object 'Haoma's heart' marked by را. زآن گزند ('from that harm') is an ablative phrase explaining cause: 'because of that harm'.
segments.s316-g96: This clause is agent–effect: بر او ('upon him' / 'against him') plus سست کرد ('made weak' — سست 'weak' + کرد 'made/caused') and the agent/predicate آن کیانی کمند ('that Kayanian snare'). Word order places the effect before the cause: 'upon him it made weak that Kayanian snare.'

sentences.s316-s21: who now, having fled into this narrow cave, has taken refuge inside the heavy enclosure.
sentences.s316-s22: When Hoom heard this cry in Turkish, he abandoned his worship and left his homeland.
sentences.s316-s23: He said thus: that this cry at the time of sleep could be none other than that Afrasiab.
sentences.s316-s24: When a thought came into his heart, he groped/checked about for a while in the dark cave.
sentences.s316-s49: He realized that the pious man granted (what was asked) because of the king's lament.
sentences.s316-s50: It turned, and withdrew itself from him; it leapt into the sea and became vanished.
sentences.s316-s5: He saw no open spot from which to take flight, nor beneath it any track for the lion and that boar.
sentences.s316-s6: He took its food, and from fear for his life he made a place; inside the cave he constructed an upper chamber.
sentences.s316-s7: It lay far from every city and near the water, where, at that time, Afrasiab summoned him.
sentences.s316-s8: He remained inside for a while, remorseful of what he had done and with his heart full of blood.
sentences.s316-s1: From that time on Afrasiab was so wretched that wherever he went he had no food or sleep.
sentences.s316-s2: He was neither safe in soul nor sound in body; always frightened, perpetually fearing harm.
sentences.s316-s3: He went about seeking a place away from the world that would render him safe in soul and sound in body.
sentences.s316-s4: Nearby, beneath a cloak, there was a certain cave; the cave on the mountain's summit had no use for the world (was untouched by the world).
sentences.s316-s17: All the Turks and the Chinese are under your command and have reached every place of your covenant.
sentences.s316-s18: You have one cave for pleasure, with a harp; whence that head, crown, and men of war?
sentences.s316-s19: Whence came all that strength and manhood, that bravery and force and wisdom?
sentences.s316-s20: Whence that greatness and throne and headgear — whence that land and homeland and so many troops?
sentences.s316-s9: When the proud head becomes bloodstained, there will be no long stay on the throne of the Kians.
sentences.s316-s10: In that age there was a certain good man, a teacher sprung from the seed of Fereydun.
sentences.s316-s11: He guarded the farr and exalted rank of the Kians; in every affair he was bound to the king.
sentences.s316-s12: His place of worship was a mountain; it was wholly given to rejoicing, distant and far from the flocks.
sentences.s316-s41: He thus drove (it) up to the high summit of heaven, so that pain, sorrow and harm might come upon me.
sentences.s316-s42: No one escapes the command of God, even if he were to entrust his eye to a dragon.
sentences.s316-s43: Pity/forgive me, for I am wretched; and even if at times I have been unjust to myself...
sentences.s316-s44: I am a scion of Fereydun the Fortunate; I will surely break away from the fetter of your snare.
sentences.s316-s37: Among the kings of the world was a brother who slew; he too met the stern judgment of the Pure God.
sentences.s316-s38: Like Aghrirath and the famed Nowzar, Siawush is a sorrowful memorial in the world.
sentences.s316-s39: Do not spill the blood of the innocent; do not flee into a rootless cave.
sentences.s316-s40: He said to him, 'Who in the world is innocent — whom do you know, O man of rank?'
sentences.s316-s13: Where was the name of this famed Hom? A worshipper lived far from hearth and homeland.
sentences.s316-s14: A certain palace stood upon that lofty mountain; it was very near to it and remote from the company.
sentences.s316-s15: A wool‑clad man who had made a place of worship — from his palm a single moan came to the ear.
sentences.s316-s16: That, O king, was Mahtara's renowned dwelling, esteemed by the great and by Daura, the judge above judges.
sentences.s316-s25: From the mountain he came out at the hour of sleep; he saw that gate in Afrasiab's court.
sentences.s316-s26: He came like a fierce lion; from his woolen cloak he opened a round space in the middle.
sentences.s316-s27: He had a noose which he wore in place of a girdle; where could he find refuge under the sovereign's protection?
sentences.s316-s28: At the moment he went inside he seized it in his hand; when he drew near he bound (tied) his arm.
sentences.s316-s29: He kept going, dragging him after into it; he rushed on with hardship, as if bereft.
sentences.s316-s30: It is strange; if you were to remain here, it would be fitting for anyone who is ruler of the world.
sentences.s316-s31: Choose nothing but a good name; one ought to take (pluck) and one ought to graze.
sentences.s316-s32: From the world choose a single cave rightly; who knows whether that cave is a time of calamity?
sentences.s316-s33: When Haoma bound that king's arm, he took him from his place and (then) sat down.
sentences.s316-s34: He said to him, 'O man, intelligent and fearful (cautious), worshipper and possessor of the pure Yazdan.'
sentences.s316-s35: What do you want from my muttering? Who are we in the world, sitting in this cave with our thoughts?
sentences.s316-s36: Haoma said to him, 'This is no rest for you; the world is throughout full of names.'
sentences.s316-s45: Where will you take me — bound and despised? Do you not fear God and the Day of Reckoning?
sentences.s316-s46: He said to him, 'Haoma — ill and mistrustful one! Truly, you have little time left.'
sentences.s316-s47: Your verses are like a fresh rose-garden; your wit is in the hand (power) of Kay Khosrow.
sentences.s316-s48: That harm turned Haoma's heart; that Kayanian snare made him weak.
